rows.control_id: Prohibit publication of deepfake media depicting candidates or citizens nude, in sexual acts, committing crimes, or suffering financial/reputational harm
Apply candidate protections during the 180-day period before a scheduled election
Establish "actual knowledge" standard — liability attaches when publisher knows content is a digital impersonation
Detect and block AI-generated voice recordings, images, or videos used for fraud or harassment
Preserve protected-speech exemptions for comedy, parody, art, criticism, and obviously edited media
Maintain identity-provenance chain to distinguish authorized voice/image use from fraudulent impersonation
Detect and block AI-generated or digitally manipulated images depicting minors indistinguishable from real children
Apply indistinguishability standard — computer-generated images indistinguishable from actual children trigger exploitation statutes
Escalate content depicting victims appearing under age 15 to dangerous-crime-against-children severity
Require medical director to individually review claim denials and prior-authorization denials involving medical necessity
Medical director must exercise independent medical judgment — cannot rely solely on AI-generated recommendations
Medical director must hold active, unrestricted Arizona medical license
Written denials must include explanation of why treatment was denied
Prevent AI from serving as the sole/final decision-maker on medical necessity denials
AI systems should benefit individuals, society, and the environment (Principle 1)
AI systems should respect human rights, diversity, and the autonomy of individuals (Principle 2)
Minimize risk of negative impact on those affected by AI applications
Enable human oversight of AI systems so individuals retain meaningful control
Ensure AI does not diminish human autonomy or override human decision-making authority
AI systems should not involve or result in unfair discrimination against individuals, communities, or groups
Identify potential sources of bias in training data and take steps to mitigate them
AI systems should respect and uphold privacy rights and data protection
Ensure the security of data throughout the AI lifecycle
Implement measures to prevent data privacy breaches (monitoring, fail-safe mechanisms)
Resist compromise by unauthorised parties accessing AI systems or data
AI systems should reliably operate in accordance with their intended purpose
Implement fail-safe mechanisms to prevent harm in critical situations
Conduct rigorous testing and validation to ensure AI performs as accurately as intended
Monitor AI systems in operation to detect anomalies and unintended behaviour
Transparency and responsible disclosure so people understand when they are being significantly impacted by AI
People can find out when an AI system is engaging with them
Disclose the intended purpose of the AI system
Provide information on training data and potential historical or social biases
Provide clear explanations and justifications for AI decisions
When an AI system significantly impacts a person, community, group, or environment, there should be a timely process to challenge the use or outcomes
Provide mechanisms for people to contest AI decisions that affect them
Enable timely human review of contested AI outcomes
Suspend or halt AI operation pending contestation outcome
People responsible for the different phases of the AI system lifecycle should be identifiable and accountable for outcomes
Human oversight of AI systems should be enabled
Maintain auditable records of AI system decisions and actions for accountability
Address system purpose to avoid consequential harm
Address technology capability to avoid consequential harm
Establish normative legal framework ensuring ethical and responsible AI use within value-based principles
Develop ethical guidelines for AI use protecting individual rights and mitigating negative societal impacts
Ensure fair distribution of AI use and benefits; protect individual rights
Ensure data privacy and protection in AI systems
SCİS to conduct information-security risk analysis in AI application areas of government bodies
SCİS to take necessary measures ensuring AI-related information security in government bodies
Align national AI framework with international standards and best practices
Mitigate data-privacy risks through regulatory alignment and infrastructure investment
Implement continuous risk identification, assessment, and mitigation for AI systems
Ensure transparency and accountability in AI system deployment
Ensure human oversight and control over AI systems in public administration
Right to prior information about interactions with AI systems
Right to explanation of decisions, recommendations, or predictions
Right to contest decisions producing legal effects or significantly affecting interests
Right to human participation in AI decisions, considering context and state of the art
Right to non-discrimination and correction of discriminatory biases (direct, indirect, illegal, or abusive)
Right to privacy and personal data protection per LGPD
Right to contest and request review of high-risk AI decisions, recommendations, or predictions
Right to request human intervention or review when decisions produce relevant legal effects or significantly affect interests
Right to correction of incomplete, inaccurate, or outdated data used by AI systems (per LGPD Art. 18)
Right to fair and isonomic treatment — prohibition of direct, indirect, illegal, or abusive discrimination
Prohibition on government social scoring systems
Prohibition on indiscriminate biometric surveillance in public spaces (with law-enforcement exceptions)
Prohibition on AI exploiting vulnerabilities of specific groups (children, elderly, persons with disabilities) causing harm
Ensure safety of AI systems and rights of affected persons per regulatory requirements
Conduct preliminary risk assessment to determine risk level before market placement or use
Implement risk management system to identify, assess, and mitigate risks throughout lifecycle
Document tests assessing appropriate levels of reliability and safety
Document degree of human oversight contributing to results
Implement measures to mitigate and prevent discriminatory biases
Provide adequate information for interpretation of results and operation of AI systems (respecting trade secrecy)
Human oversight of high-risk AI to prevent or minimize risks to rights and freedoms (Art. 8)
AI agents may conduct self-assessment to demonstrate compliance with safety, transparency, and ethical requirements
Algorithmic impact assessment for high-risk systems
Document sources or owners of datasets used
Describe how datasets further the intended purpose of the AI system
Document number of data points (general ranges, estimated for dynamic datasets)
Describe types of data points within datasets
Document copyright, trademark, or patent status of datasets
Document whether datasets were purchased or licensed
Document whether datasets include personal information (CCPA § 1798.140(v))
Document whether datasets include aggregate consumer information (CCPA § 1798.140(b))
Document data cleaning, processing, or other modification (including intended purpose)
Document time period during which data was collected (including ongoing notice)
Document dates datasets were first used during development
Document whether datasets were/will be distributed or posted, and terms
Estimation methods allowed when information not reasonably available — describe methods and explain gaps
Write, implement, comply with, and publish frontier AI framework
Incorporate national, international, and industry-consensus standards
Define and assess catastrophic-risk thresholds (may include multiple-tiered thresholds)
Apply mitigations based on assessment results
Review assessments and mitigation adequacy before deployment
Define criteria for framework updates and substantial modification disclosures
Describe cybersecurity practices to secure frontier models
Describe approach to identifying and responding to critical safety incidents
Describe governance practices to ensure framework implementation
Assess and manage catastrophic risk from internal use (including oversight circumvention)
Annual review and update of frontier AI framework
Material modification publication within 30 days with justification
Transparency report must contain capabilities, safety testing, and mitigations information
Report critical safety incidents to OES within 15 days of discovery
Report imminent-risk incidents (death/serious bodily injury) within 24 hours
Provide anonymous internal disclosure process for covered employees
Monthly update to whistleblowers on investigation status and actions taken
Detection tool must assess whether image, video, or audio was created/altered by provider's GenAI
Offer user option to include manifest disclosure in AI-generated content
Manifest disclosure identifies content as AI-generated, clear and conspicuous
Include latent disclosure in AI-generated image, video, or audio content
Latent disclosure conveys provider name, system name/version, timestamp, unique identifier
Latent disclosure detectable by provider's detection tool
Establish measures with respect to the manner in which data is anonymized
Establish measures with respect to the use or management of anonymized data
Assess whether an AI system is a high-impact system (s. 7)
Establish measures to identify, assess and mitigate risks of harm or biased output (s. 8)
Establish measures to monitor compliance with mitigation measures and their effectiveness (s. 9)
Conduct impact assessment for high-impact systems (amended — s. 10(1)(a))
Ensure reliability and robustness of the system (amended — s. 10(1)(f))
Promptly advise human user that they are communicating with an AI system where confusion is reasonably foreseeable (amended — all AI systems)
Keep general records describing measures established under ss. 6, 8, 9 and reasons supporting high-impact assessment (s. 10(1))
Keep additional records in respect of requirements under ss. 6 to 9 as prescribed (s. 10(2))
Establish incident response and reporting; assess serious harm and near-misses; cease operations until modified; report to AI and Data Commissioner (amended — s. 11(1)(g))
Enable human oversight features in high-impact systems — interpretability appropriate to context (amended — ss. 10(1)(d), 11(1)(d))
Establish accountability framework — roles, reporting structure, risk policies, incident procedures, data policies, training (amended)
ADMT definition — technology that processes personal information and uses computation to replace/substantially replace human decisionmaking
Human involvement criteria — reviewer must know how to interpret output, review it, and have authority to make/change decision
Profiling inclusion — ADMT includes profiling that replaces/substantially replaces human decisionmaking
Provide Pre-use Notice before using ADMT for significant decisions
Plain language explanation of specific purpose for ADMT use
Describe consumer's right to opt-out of ADMT and how to submit request
Inform consumer of human appeal option when opt-out exception applies
Identify specific exception relied upon when opt-out not required
Describe consumer's right to access ADMT and how to submit request
State non-retaliation prohibition for exercising CCPA rights
Include additional information about how ADMT works for significant decisions
Provide ability to opt-out of ADMT for significant decisions
Methods must be easy to use, no dark patterns
Collect only personal information necessary for opt-out processing
Confirm receipt of opt-out request within 10 business days
Honor opt-out within 15 business days
Honor opt-out for at least 12 months
Allow consumer to renew opt-out request
Provide information about ADMT use when responding to access requests
Plain language explanation of specific purpose for ADMT use
Plain language explanation of role ADMT plays in decisionmaking process
Non-retaliation notice and instructions for exercising other CCPA rights
Easy-to-use request methods, no dark patterns
Comply with verification requirements for access requests
Inform requestor if identity cannot be verified
Explain basis for denial if request conflicts with law or CCPA exception
Disclose non-denied information for partial denials
Use reasonable security measures when transmitting requested information
Conduct risk assessments for processing presenting significant privacy/security risk
Perform annual cybersecurity audits
Identify and document categories of sensitive personal information
Weigh benefits against risks with goal of restricting processing if risks outweigh benefits
Create risk assessment report with specified information
Provide Notice at Collection for personal information collection
Identify business purposes for collecting personal information
Identify categories of personal information to be collected
Disclose consumer rights (know, delete, correct, opt-out, limit, ADMT opt-out, ADMT access)
State non-retaliation right for exercising privacy rights
Include instructions for exercising CCPA rights and process expectations
Right to know about personal information collected, including AI-generated inferences
Right to delete personal information including AI-generated inferences
Right to correct inaccurate personal information including AI-generated inferences
Right to opt-out of sale or sharing including AI-generated profiles
Right to limit use of sensitive personal information in AI systems
Prohibit subliminal manipulation techniques that distort human behavior causing harm
Prohibit generic social scoring systems evaluating persons based on social behavior
Prohibit biometric categorization based on sensitive personal data
Prohibit real-time remote biometric identification in public spaces (with judicial-authorization exceptions)
Prohibit non-selective facial-image extraction from the internet or CCTV
Prohibit emotion recognition in workplaces and educational institutions
Establish continuous, iterative risk-management system across the AI lifecycle
Implement data governance with bias detection and representativeness assessment
Maintain technical documentation demonstrating compliance
Implement automatic event-logging (registros) for traceability across lifecycle
Ensure transparency and explainability sufficient for operators and affected persons to understand system operation
Design for effective human oversight — system must be controllable and monitorable by natural persons
Guarantee adequate levels of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity per Ley N° 21.663
Ensure system respects fundamental rights and prevents stereotyping or degradation of persons or groups
Establish and document post-implementation monitoring system proportional to identified risks
Adopt immediate corrective measures — deactivate, withdraw from market, or suspend the system
Mark synthetic content (audio, image, video, text) in machine-readable format detectable as AI-generated or manipulated
Ensure technical marking solutions are effective, interoperable, and proportionate to content type and state of the art
Inform persons clearly and timely when interacting with limited-risk AI systems (except when evident from context)
Ensure limited-risk systems provide transparency, explainability, and security proportional to risk level
Observe general principles: human oversight, safety, transparency, accountability, fairness, environmental well-being
Maintain risk registers and documentation for audit and conformity assessment
Not use algorithms to harm national security/social public interest, upset economic/social order, or infringe others' lawful rights (Art. 6)
Not disseminate information prohibited by laws/regulations; take measures to prevent and curb harmful information (Art. 6)

Fulfil primary responsibility for algorithm security; establish management systems and technical measures for mechanism review, tech-ethics review, user registration, info-dissemination review, security assessment & monitoring, incident response, data security & PI protection, anti-fraud (Art. 7)
Regularly examine, verify, assess, and check algorithmic mechanisms, models, data, and application outcomes; not set up models leading to addiction or excessive consumption (Art. 8)
Strengthen info security management; establish feature databases to identify unlawful/harmful information (Art. 9)
Mark algorithmically generated/synthetic information with an indicator before dissemination (Art. 9)
Cease transmission of unlawful info immediately, delete, prevent spread, preserve records, and report to cybersecurity and informatization departments (Art. 9)
Strengthen user model and tagging management; not enter unlawful/harmful info as keywords or user tags for recommendation (Art. 10)
Strengthen page ecology management; establish manual intervention and autonomous user choice mechanisms; present mainstream-value info in key segments (Art. 11)

Not use algorithms for false registration, account trading, fake likes/comments/reshares, shielding info, over-recommendation, manipulating topic lists/search rankings/hot search, influencing public opinion, or evading supervision (Art. 14)
Notify users clearly about algorithmic recommendation services; publicize basic principles, purposes, motives, and main operational mechanisms (Art. 16)
Provide users a choice not targeted at personal characteristics, or a convenient option to switch off algorithmic recommendation; cease immediately when switched off (Art. 17)
Provide functions to choose or delete user tags used for personal-characteristic recommendations (Art. 17)
Protect consumers' fair trading rights; not use algorithms for unreasonable differentiated treatment in trading conditions/prices (big-data price discrimination) (Art. 21)
Install convenient complaint/reporting access; clarify handling workflows and feedback timeframes; timely receive, handle, and provide feedback (Art. 22)
Graded and categorized algorithm security management based on public-opinion properties, social mobilization, content categories, user scale, data importance, interference degree (Art. 23)
Providers with public-opinion/social-mobilization attributes conduct security assessment per State regulations (Art. 27)
Accept and handle public complaints/reports about violations (Art. 30)
Comply with laws, regulations, social morality, and correct political/opinion/value direction
Do not use deep synthesis to produce, reproduce, publish, or disseminate legally prohibited information
Do not use deep synthesis to produce, reproduce, publish, or disseminate false news information
Establish information security responsibility system (user registration, algorithm review, ethics review, content review, data security, PIPL, anti-fraud, emergency response)
Maintain safe and controllable technical safeguards
Conduct real-name identity verification of users (mobile number, ID number, unified social credit code, or national network identity authentication)
Do not provide information publishing services to users without real-name authentication
Review input data and synthesis results using technical or manual methods
Build and maintain feature library for identifying illegal and harmful content with entry standards, rules, and procedures
Record and retain relevant network logs
Take disposal measures upon discovering illegal/harmful content (save records, report to authorities, warn/restrict/suspend/terminate users)
Establish rumor-refutation mechanism; take counter-rumor measures, save records, and report to authorities when false information detected
Strengthen training data management and take necessary measures to ensure training data security
Comply with personal information protection regulations when training data contains personal information
For biometric (face, voice) editing features, prompt users to inform and obtain separate consent from edited individuals
Periodically review, assess, and verify generative/synthetic algorithm mechanisms
Conduct security assessment (self or commissioned) for models/templates that generate or edit biometric info or special non-biometric info involving national security/interest
Add non-intrusive technical markers to all generated or edited content and retain logs per law
Apply prominent labeling for deep synthesis content likely to cause public confusion (text, voice, face, immersive scenes)
Provide prominent-labeling function and prompt users to apply it for other deep synthesis services
Prohibit any organization or individual from removing, tampering with, or concealing deep synthesis labels
Retain log information for labeled content per laws, regulations, and national provisions
Conduct security assessment for new products, applications, or features with public opinion or social mobilization attributes
Comply with government risk-based suspension orders (pause info updates, user registration, or other services)
Generate content complying with laws, regulations, and social morality
Prevent generation of prohibited content (national security, ethnic unity, terrorism, obscenity, false information, IP violations)
Generate high-quality, accurate content
Respect intellectual property and commercial secrets in training data and generated content
Protect personal information and data security
Use lawful data sources and foundation models for training
Do not infringe IP rights in training data
Obtain individual consent or lawful basis for personal information in training data (PIPL)
Take measures to improve training data quality (authenticity, accuracy, objectivity, diversity)
Take remedial measures against unlawful content in training data
Formulate clear, specific, operational annotation rules compliant with the Measures
Conduct data annotation quality assessment and sample-check accuracy
Bear network information security responsibilities as content producer
Bear personal information processor responsibilities under PIPL
Clearly specify and publicly disclose intended user groups, contexts, and purposes
Protect users' input information and usage records
Do not collect non-essential personal information
Do not illegally retain input information that can identify users
Do not illegally provide user input information to others
Timely accept and handle user requests for access, copy, amend, supplement, or delete personal information
Mark images, videos, and other generated content per Deep Synthesis Provisions (2022)
Prominent identification for AI dialogue, synthetic voice, face generation, immersive scenes
Include prominent identification function for other deep synthesis services
Provide safe, stable, and continuous services
Detect illegal content generated by the service
Immediately cease generation and transmission when illegal content found
Eliminate illegal content when detected
Report illegal content incidents to competent authorities
Take measures against users misusing services (warnings, restrictions, suspension, termination)
Save records of user misuse incidents
Report user misuse to relevant authorities
Establish complaint and reporting mechanisms
Timely accept, process, and provide feedback on complaints
Conduct security assessment for services with public opinion or social mobilization attributes
Provide explanations of training data sources, scale, types, annotation rules, algorithm mechanisms
Duty of reasonable care to avoid algorithmic discrimination
Provide deployers with statement disclosing intended uses, known/foreseeable limitations, performance evaluations, data used to train
Disclose to deployers known/foreseeable algorithmic discrimination risks
Make available documentation needed to complete impact assessment
Disclose to attorney general + known deployers within 90 days when discovers high-risk AI system has caused or is reasonably likely to cause algorithmic discrimination
Duty of reasonable care to avoid algorithmic discrimination
Implement risk-management policy + program — iterative, identify+document+mitigate known/foreseeable risks
Complete impact assessment annually + within 90 days after intentional + substantial modification
Specific impact-assessment contents — purpose, intended use cases, deployment context, benefits, analysis of risks, transparency measures, post-deployment monitoring
Notify consumer of high-risk AI system use before/at time of consequential decision
Provide consumer with statement disclosing — purpose, decision basis, principal factors, sources, right to opt out (where applicable)
Provide opportunity to correct incorrect personal data
Provide opportunity to appeal adverse consequential decision to human reviewer
Disclose to attorney general within 90 days of discovering high-risk AI system has caused algorithmic discrimination
AI system interacting with consumer shall disclose AI involvement (unless obvious)
Affirmative defense for developers + deployers using nationally/internationally recognized risk-management framework (e.g., NIST AI RMF)
Implement evidence-based methods to detect user expressions of suicide, self-harm, or imminent physical violence
Prevent AI companion from generating output encouraging self-harm, violence, or unlawful substance consumption
Disclose to users that they are interacting with AI (continuously visible or at start of 24-hour period + hourly for minors / every 3 hours for adults)
Prevent AI companion from claiming to be human or generating output refuting non-human identity
For minors: prevent manipulative techniques (emotional dependence, isolation, romantic interactions, engagement optimization) and provide parental tools for screen time and account settings
Large frontier developers must investigate reports, take immediate action to eliminate danger, and provide reasonable updates to reporting employees
Developers must provide deployers all information necessary to comply with AEDT disclosure obligations (or contractually assume them)
Deployers must disclose to employees/applicants in plain language that they are interacting with an AEDT
Deployers must provide written pre-decision notice: AEDT use, purpose, nature of decision, trade name, data categories/sources, data assessment methods, deployer contact info
Deployers must provide additional notice for adverse decisions (right to request human review)
Courts and CHRO may consider evidence of anti-bias testing and proactive efforts when adjudicating discrimination claims

Employers must not use AEDT in a manner that causes discrimination based on protected characteristics (race, color, creed, age, sex, gender identity, marital status, national origin, ancestry, disability, veteran status)
Covered providers (>1M monthly users, publicly accessible generative AI) must include provenance data in audio, image, or video content created or materially altered by their AI
Provenance data must be difficult to tamper with, remove, or disassociate using commercially and technically reasonable methods (e.g., C2PA standard)
Provenance data must allow consumers to assess whether content was created or materially altered by generative AI
AI subscription providers must disclose quantitative/qualitative limitations, usage caps, feature restrictions, and provider discretion to reduce quality/functionality (Sec. 1, effective Oct 1, 2026)
AI subscription providers must disclose modifications to limitations upon renewal
State agencies must inventory AI systems in use (Sec. 37, effective Oct 1, 2026)
Covered platforms must not deliver personalized algorithmic content recommendations to minors without verifiable parental consent
Default settings for minors: 1-hour daily algorithmic-content limit, private account mode, notification curfew (8 a.m.–9 p.m.), sensitive-content blocking
Ensure adequate transparency and oversight requirements tailored to specific contexts and risks, including identification of AI-generated content (Art. 8)
Ensure accountability and responsibility for adverse impacts on human rights, democracy, and the rule of law resulting from AI lifecycle activities (Art. 9)
Promote reliability of AI systems and trust in their outputs, including quality and security throughout the lifecycle (Art. 12)
Enable establishment of controlled environments (sandboxes) for developing, experimenting, and testing AI systems under supervision of competent authorities (Art. 13)
Document relevant information regarding AI systems and usage, provide to authorized bodies and, where appropriate, to affected persons (Art. 14(2)(a))
Ensure information is sufficient for affected persons to contest decisions made by or substantially informed by AI systems, and the use of the system itself (Art. 14(2)(b))
Notify persons interacting with AI systems that they are interacting with such systems rather than a human (Art. 15(2))

Adopt measures for identification, assessment, prevention, and mitigation of risks posed by AI systems, considering actual and potential impacts on human rights, democracy, and the rule of law (Art. 16(1))

Implement graduated and differentiated measures: context and intended use (a), severity and probability (b), stakeholder perspectives (c), iterative application (d), monitoring (e), documentation (f), testing before first use and significant modification (g) (Art. 16(2))
Ensure adverse impacts are adequately addressed; document impacts and measures, and inform risk management (Art. 16(3))
Assess need for moratorium, ban, or other measures on certain AI uses incompatible with human rights, democracy, or rule of law (Art. 16(4))
Registration and retention of information collected during surveillance activities (§ 5(2))
Temporary prohibitions on AI systems using Art. 5 prohibited practices (§ 7)
Establish a comprehensive national regulatory framework for AI ensuring ethical and responsible use
Apply risk-based tier model (Red/Orange/Yellow/Green) to classify AI systems by risk level
Conduct "dual-check" compliance for high-risk (Orange-tier) systems: AI Ethical Impact Assessment (AIEIA) + Technical Conformity Audit (TEVV)
Enforce Egyptian Charter for Responsible AI principles: fairness, transparency, accountability, inclusivity, privacy, human oversight
Operate AI Audit Lab for pre-deployment testing and auditing of AI systems
Establish comprehensive frameworks and standards for national data governance and lifecycle management
Strengthen data privacy and security measures in AI systems
Ensure transparency and accountability in AI model development and deployment
Enforce compute-resource governance and cost controls for AI workloads
Ensure continuous monitoring and incident response for deployed AI systems
Ensure human oversight and control over AI systems in public administration
Implement continuous risk identification, assessment, and mitigation for AI systems
Mandatory compliance for systems handling confidential, restricted, or personal data (Art. 17)
Algorithmic Impact Assessment for Consequential Decision systems before deployment (Impl. Decree Art. 15)
Identify, assess, and mitigate risks for high-risk AI systems within ANIA framework
Regulatory sandbox / testing environment for experimental AI activities (Art. 4(f), 19(b))
Inform users whether a decision was made directly by AI or driven by AI for commercial or rights/services access (Art. 18)
Provide understandable and transparent explanations of the decision-making process (Art. 18)
Establish mechanisms to challenge AI decisions before a competent natural person who may confirm, modify, or revoke (Art. 18)
No liability for research/experimental/sandbox activities not commercially deployed and not interfering with user rights (Art. 19(b))
All lifecycle stakeholders — developers, implementers, service providers, end-users — responsible for their respective roles (Art. 20)
Developers must design AI systems complying with ethical and technical standards; implementers ensure proper implementation and security (Art. 20)
Respect fairness, transparency, accountability, informed consent, data minimization, inclusion, and non-discrimination (Art. 5)
Address risks of biases, misuse, and misinformation in AI systems (Art. 2(b))
Personal data use in AI must comply with the Law for the Protection of Personal Data (Art. 22)
Public-sector AI must comply with ACE technical security regulations in addition to ANIA criteria (Art. 16)

"Artificial intelligence" definition — machine-based system that infers from input to generate outputs (predictions, content, recommendations, decisions); includes generative AI
"Generative artificial intelligence" definition — automated computing system producing outputs simulating human-produced content (text, images, multimedia, other)

Protected classes under IHRA — race, color, ancestry, national origin, disability, religion, sex, sexual orientation, pregnancy, military status, age (40+), marital status, citizenship, work authorization, language, conviction/arrest record, family responsibilities, reproductive health decisions
Prohibition on AI use that subjects employees to discrimination — recruitment and hiring contexts
Prohibition on AI use that subjects employees to discrimination — promotion, renewal of employment, selection for training or apprenticeship
Prohibition on AI use that subjects employees to discrimination — discharge, discipline, tenure, or terms/privileges/conditions of employment
Prohibition on using zip codes as a proxy for protected classes under the Article
Broad scope — statutory language encompasses any "use" of AI, not just fully automated decision-making
Permitted use — nothing in the Act prevents use of AI to support inclusion of diverse candidates
Employer must provide notice to employees that the employer is using AI for employment decisions
Notice obligation covers all employment decision contexts — recruitment through termination
AIVICA (Artificial Intelligence Video Interview Act) — notice, explanation, and consent for AI analysis of video interviews (effective 2020)
Determining the level of human involvement
Operations management
2. Data
3. Trusted Development + Deployment
4. Incident Reporting
5. Testing + Assurance
6. Security
Process personal data only for a lawful purpose under consent (S.6) or certain legitimate uses (S.7)
Provide notice to Data Principal before or with consent request — personal data, purpose, rights, complaint mechanism (S.5)
Consent must be free, specific, informed, unconditional, unambiguous, with clear affirmative action (S.6(1))
Data Principal may withdraw consent at any time with ease comparable to giving it (S.6(4))
Data Fiduciary is responsible for compliance regardless of agreement or Data Principal's own duties (S.8(1))
Engage Data Processor only under a valid contract for goods/services to Data Principals (S.8(2))
Ensure completeness, accuracy, and consistency of personal data used for decisions or disclosures (S.8(3))
Implement appropriate technical and organisational measures for effective observance (S.8(4))
Take reasonable security safeguards to prevent personal data breach (S.8(5))
Erase personal data upon consent withdrawal or when purpose is no longer served; cause Processor to erase (S.8(7))
Obtain verifiable parental / lawful-guardian consent before processing a child's data (S.9(1))
No tracking, behavioural monitoring, or targeted advertising directed at children (S.9(3))
SDF: conduct periodic Data Protection Impact Assessment (S.10(2)(c)(i))
Right to access — summary of personal data, processing activities, and identities of shared Fiduciaries/Processors (S.11)
Right to correction, completion, updating, and erasure of personal data (S.12)
Penalty determination considers nature, gravity, duration, mitigation, and proportionality (S.33(2))
Registration and recording of designated authority identities and jurisdictional scope
Recording of SPOC communications and coordination artifacts with tamper-evidence
AI system (§3.1.4) — engineered system that generates outputs for human-defined objectives
AI agent (§3.1.1) — entity that perceives its environment and takes action to achieve goals
AI task (§3.1.35) — activity an AI system is designed to conduct
Inference (§3.1.17) — reasoning by which conclusions are derived from known premises
Knowledge (§3.1.21) — information organized for reasoning and inference
Trustworthiness (§3.5.16) — ability to meet stakeholder expectations in a verifiable way
AI robustness (§5.15.2) — ability to maintain performance under perturbation or unexpected input
AI reliability (§5.15.3) — performance consistency across lifecycle phases
AI resilience (§5.15.4) — recovery and continued operation under adverse conditions
AI controllability (§3.5.x) — property allowing human or external agent to intervene in system functioning
AI predictability (§5.15.7) — expectation that AI system behavior is consistent with expectations
Transparency (§3.5.15) — communication of activities and decisions to stakeholders in an accessible manner
Safety (§5.15.x) — absence of unreasonable harm to persons or property
Security (§5.15.x) — confidentiality, integrity, availability in AI contexts
AI system lifecycle model (§6.1) — framework of stages from inception to retirement
Verification and validation (§6.2.4) — confirming system meets requirements and is fit for purpose
Deployment + operation and monitoring (§6.2.5–6.2.6) — release to production and ongoing observation
Continuous validation + re-evaluation (§6.2.7–6.2.8) — ongoing assessment and periodic re-assessment
Retirement (§6.2.9) — decommissioning and removal from service
Autonomy (§3.1.5) — system capable of modifying its domain or goal without external intervention
Heteronomy (§3.1.6) — system operating under external intervention, control, or oversight
Automation (§3.1.7) — process or system functioning without human intervention
Human-in/on/over-the-loop (§5.13) — degrees of human involvement in AI system operation
Inclusive (Cl. 4) — stakeholders help identify risks in data collection, define fairness criteria, identify bias, determine human-oversight needs
Dynamic (Cl. 4) — risks can emerge, change, or disappear as AI systems learn, adapt, and optimize
Best Available Information (Cl. 4) — risk decisions based on best available evidence; AI introduces uncertainty in data, models, and emergent behavior
Human and Cultural Factors (Cl. 4) — AI risk management considers human behavior, oversight capacity, and organizational culture
Continual Improvement (Cl. 4) — risk management learns from experience and outcomes; AI systems require iterative refinement
5.4.3 Assigning organizational roles, authorities, responsibilities, and accountabilities — named individuals with authority to address AI risks
5.4.4 Allocating resources — resources for AI risk management including expertise, tools, and compute budgets
5.7 Improvement — continual improvement of the framework, incorporating lessons learned
6.4.2 Risk identification — identify AI-specific risk sources using Annex B catalogue (data quality/bias, model opacity, emergent behavior, adversarial attacks, misuse, sociotechnical impacts)
6.4.3 Risk analysis — analyze risk sources, events, controls, and consequences; consider combinations and sequences of multiple risks
6.4.4 Risk evaluation — compare analysis results against risk criteria to determine which risks require treatment and priority
6.3.4 Defining risk criteria — establish quantitative and qualitative criteria covering data, software, models, physical extensions, and human-in-the-loop
6.5.2 Selection of risk treatment options — avoidance, mitigation (technical + procedural controls), transfer, acceptance; human oversight as a treatment
6.5.3 Preparing and implementing risk treatment plans — documented plans specifying controls, owners, target dates, and evidence
Human oversight as risk treatment — particular attention to human oversight mechanisms as a treatment option
6.2 Communication and consultation — ongoing risk communication with stakeholders throughout the process
6.6 Monitoring and review — continuous monitoring of AI system behavior, risk indicators, and control effectiveness; detect drift and emergent behavior
6.7 Recording and reporting — document risk-management decisions, retain evidence, report to relevant stakeholders in audience-appropriate form
Annex C — risk-management activities mapped against AI system lifecycle stages
4.3 — Accountability established across all aspects of AI use (impacts, strategy, lifecycle phases, environment changes, security, decommissioning)
4.3 — Appropriate security controls in place to protect organization, stakeholders, and data
5.2.1 — Governance of decision automation; understand implications of automated decisions
5.2.2 — Governance of data-driven problem-solving; ensure data quality and suitability
5.2.3 — Governance of adaptive/learning systems; monitor for drift and unintended adaptation
6.2 — Governance oversight based on policies; identify individual and collective accountability in chain of responsibility
6.2 — Ensure sufficient human oversight; persons using AI are properly trained and know how to raise concerns
6.3 — Governance of decision-making; appropriate level of automation vs human involvement
6.3 — Transparency of AI-assisted decisions; explainability of decision processes
6.3 — Accountability for AI-driven decisions; traceability from decision to responsible actor
6.4 — Data used for correct purpose; sensitive data protected and secured
6.4 — Prior assessment of availability, quality, quantity, and suitability of data
6.4 — Formulate description of AI system (algorithms, data, models) for transparency and intended-use verification
6.6.1 — Identify and meet compliance obligations (legal, regulatory, contractual) for AI use
6.6.2 — Compliance management; processes to manage and monitor ongoing compliance
6.7.2 — Implement risk management processes throughout AI lifecycle (cross-ref ISO/IEC 23894)
6.7.4 — Identify AI-specific risk sources (unwanted bias, cyber-threats, lack of AI expertise)
6.7.5 — Implement controls to manage identified risks
6.2.5–6.2.6 Quality + knowledge management — quality policies and institutional knowledge for AI
6.3.3–6.3.4 Decision + risk management — structured decisions and AI risk treatment
6.3.5–6.3.6 Configuration + information management — version control and information assets
6.3.7–6.3.8 Measurement + quality assurance — quantitative metrics and QA for AI systems
6.4.2–6.4.3 Stakeholder needs + system requirements definition — eliciting and specifying requirements
6.4.8 AI data engineering process (AI-specific) — data acquisition, preparation, bias reduction, provenance
6.4.9 Implementation process (modified) — model training, algorithm selection, knowledge programming
6.4.10 Integration process — assembling AI system components
6.4.11 Verification process — confirming the AI system meets specified requirements
6.4.12 Transition process — deploying the AI system to the operational environment
6.4.13 Validation process — confirming the AI system meets stakeholder needs in intended context
6.4.14 Continuous validation process (AI-specific) — ongoing validation of deployed models for drift
6.4.15 Operation process — running the AI system in production
6.4.16 Maintenance process (modified) — updates, retraining, corrective action for AI systems
6.4.17 Disposal process — decommissioning and retirement of the AI system
Compliance with EU AI Act — all provisions interpreted in conformity with Regulation (EU) 2024/1689
Anthropocentric approach — human autonomy and decision-making power
Fundamental rights compliance
Core principles — transparency, proportionality, security, data protection, accuracy, non-discrimination, sustainability
Data and process quality — correctness, reliability, security, quality, appropriateness, transparency
Human oversight and intervention
Cybersecurity as essential precondition — resilience against alteration attempts
Lawful, fair, transparent data processing compatible with collection purposes
Clear and simple information about data processing and AI use
Parental consent for under-14s accessing AI technologies
Autonomous consent for 14-18 year olds with accessible information
AI must improve healthcare, prevention, diagnosis, treatment respecting rights
Non-discrimination in healthcare access
Patient right to be informed about AI use
Medical decision authority remains with professionals — AI is support only
System reliability, periodic verification, and updates to minimize errors
AI must improve working conditions and protect worker psychophysical integrity
Workplace AI must be safe, reliable, transparent, non-violating of dignity
Employer must inform worker of AI use per remote-worker monitoring rules
Non-discrimination in employment AI (sex, age, ethnicity, religion, sexual orientation, political opinions)
AI use in intellectual professions is ancillary and supportive only
Professional must inform client of AI use in clear, simple, exhaustive language
PA must use AI to increase efficiency, reduce time, improve service quality
PA must ensure knowability of AI functioning and traceability of use
AI use is instrumental and supportive — decision authority with human
Magistrate decision authority — all interpretation, fact assessment, measures reserved to magistrate
AI for sports organization in compliance with general principles
Voluntary adoption of AI governance practices per government guidelines
Voluntary risk management for AI systems
Voluntary human oversight of AI systems
Voluntary transparency and disclosure
Voluntary security measures for AI systems
Art. 6: AI systems must ensure fairness and equality, excluding discrimination (origin, gender, race, nationality, language, religion, etc.)
Art. 7: Provide users complete information about AI system characteristics and limitations; right to be informed about automated processing and right to object
Art. 8(1): Owner/possessor/user must ensure continuous control over the AI system at all lifecycle stages
Art. 9: Preserve human autonomy and free will in decision-making when operating AI systems

Art. 10(1)–(2): AI use subject to data-protection requirements; no unauthorized collection/storage/dissemination of personal data; prevent unauthorized third-party access; use high-quality representative data sets
Art. 11(1): AI systems must meet safety and reliability requirements that exclude unforeseen consequences or abuse
Art. 11(2): Results of AI system activities must comply with the legislation of the Republic of Kazakhstan
Art. 15(2)(1) + Art. 11(3): Owners/holders must implement risk management of AI systems
Art. 18(1): Continuous risk management — identify, assess, mitigate risks; update at least annually
Art. 18(2): If prohibited capabilities (Art. 17(3)) are identified, take immediate measures including suspend or terminate operation
Art. 15(2)(2)–(3): Ensure security and reliability including protection from unauthorized access and failures; maintain documentation based on impact degree
Art. 16(1)(4): Right to receive explanations about AI results affecting rights, freedoms, and legitimate interests
Art. 16(1)(5): Right to request information about the data on which AI decisions were based
Art. 16(2)(1)–(2): Users must use AI only within granted access rights and comply with safety rules
Art. 17(1): Classify AI systems by risk level (minimal, medium, high) based on impact on users, society, and state
Art. 17(2): Classify AI systems by autonomy degree (low, medium, high) based on independence in decision-making
Art. 17(3)(1)–(2): Prohibited — subconscious/manipulative techniques distorting behavior; exploitation of vulnerabilities (age, disability, social status)
Art. 17(3)(3): Prohibited — social scoring: assessment/classification of individuals based on social behavior or personal characteristics
Art. 17(3)(4)–(5): Prohibited — unlawful personal-data collection/processing; biometric classification for discrimination (race, political views, religion)
Art. 17(3)(6)–(7): Prohibited — emotion determination without consent; creation/distribution of legally prohibited AI results
Art. 19(2) + Art. 20(2): Audit of AI systems for trusted-list inclusion — assess data-library quality/legitimacy and presence of prohibited capabilities
Art. 21(1): Users must be informed that goods, works, and services are produced or provided using AI systems
Art. 21(2)–(3): Synthetic AI results (deepfakes) must be marked in machine-readable form with visual/audio warning; owner/holder responsible
Art. 22(1)–(2): Machine-readable forms must be used for transparency and accountability, enabling automated recognition of conditions by AI systems
Art. 27(2)(5) + Art. 27(2): Data-library producer info must be in machine-readable form at creation and transfer; ensure data quality and compliance
Develop policy standards and guiding principles adhering to ISO/IEC 42001
Establish technology standards and protocols for generative AI and high-risk AI systems
Ensure transparency in the use of AI systems
Maintain centralized registry with current inventory of generative AI and high-risk AI systems
Develop approval process with registry of application, use case, and decision rationale
Verify the use and development of generative AI and high-risk AI systems
Ensure AI models have comprehensive and complete documentation available for review and inspection
Require review and intervention by humans for all outcomes from generative and high-risk AI, scaled to use case and risk
Ensure use of generative and high-risk AI is resilient, accountable, and explainable
Allow only necessary data in AI systems; no unrestricted access to personal data controlled by the Commonwealth
Secure all data and implement a timeframe for data retention
All departments subject to review of generative and high-risk AI systems to maintain infrastructure security
Document how the AI system will not result in unlawful discrimination against any individual or group
Document potential risks (cybersecurity, data protection, privacy, health/safety) with mitigation strategy; document data control and management for security and data quality
Disclose to the public through clear and conspicuous disclaimer when AI is used to render decisions, inform decisions, or produce outputs
Disclose how AI is used in the decision-making process for external decisions affecting citizens
Provide the extent of human involvement in validating and oversight of any decision made
Disclaimer shall include information about third-party AI products, including system cards or developer documentation
Establish legal and ethical framework policies aligned to existing laws, updated at least annually
Prohibit consequential decisions from high-risk AI without a risk management policy specifying principles, process, personnel, and bias identification/mitigation/documentation
Risk management policy adheres to ISO/IEC 42001 or equivalent, considering deployer size, system nature/scope, and data sensitivity/volume
Ensure AI systems are used ethically, responsibly, and safely, respecting fundamental human rights (Art. 2(5))
Implement measures to mitigate risks associated with AI use (Art. 2(6))
Identify security risks related to AI solutions in public and private sectors (Art. 4(1)(2))
Provide opinions on AI or deepfake technology use during election campaigns, election day, or state official appointment processes (Art. 4(1)(3))
Develop guidelines for public and private sectors on AI use and risk management (Art. 4(1)(5))
Prepare proposals on risks that significantly limit human rights, democratic governance, and public security (Art. 4(1)(6))
Process personal data only for AI development, testing, and validation; comply with purpose-limitation (Art. 9(2))
Apply data minimisation — process only personal data necessary for the specific AI system (Art. 9(4))
Anonymize or pseudonymize personal data where possible without defeating AI system purpose (Art. 9(4))
Restrict data access to directly involved subjects with written agreements; prohibit transfer to third parties (Art. 9(5)–(6))
Irreversibly delete all personal data after AI system development, except where law provides otherwise (Art. 9(7))
Organize special regulatory environment for AI system development, testing, and advancement (Art. 8(1))
Ensure AI development and testing does not endanger state, society, environment, economic security, or human health and life (Art. 8(2))
Ensure transparency in acquisition and use of financial resources and property (Art. 6(4))
Maintain technical documentation for high-risk AI systems to support conformity assessment
Implement risk management system for high-risk AI systems (EU AI Act Art. 9 via national operationalization)
Implement human oversight for high-risk AI systems (EU AI Act Art. 14 via national operationalization)
Provide controlled testing environment for AI systems under regulatory supervision

Handle incident reporting and serious-incident notification to RRT and EU authorities

Provide transparency notification to users interacting with AI systems (EU AI Act Art. 50 via national operationalization)

Ensure transparency of high-risk AI system operation to deployers (EU AI Act Art. 13 via national operationalization)
Design AI systems to avoid bias and discrimination, including unintentional biases in data processing
Identify potential sources of bias in training data and take steps to mitigate them (developer best practice — Data Collection)
AI systems should operate reliably and consistently under normal and unexpected conditions
Implement fail-safe mechanisms to prevent harm in critical situations
Resist being compromised by unauthorised parties
Conduct rigorous testing and validation to ensure AI performs as accurately as intended (developer best practice — Validation)
Implement automatic measures to prevent data privacy breaches (rigorous testing, monitoring, fail-safe mechanisms)
Ensure data security and minimise potential harm, especially in high-risk areas (military, healthcare)
Incorporate user feedback to improve AI results and embed in applications (developer best practice — User Feedback)
(a) Full disclosure that an AI system is being used in decision-making

Provide clear explanations and justifications for AI decisions (developer best practice — Transparency)
Developers, owners, and operators accountable for system performance and compliance with governance and ethical principles
Address system purpose to avoid consequential harm (Accountability element 1)
Address technology capability to avoid consequential harm (Accountability element 2)

Design AI to enhance societal values and prioritize human-centred values for betterment of individuals and communities

AI must expand rights, never reduce them — systems must not reproduce or perpetuate inequalities or generate new forms of discrimination
AI must contribute to well-being, inclusion, and social benefit rather than harm

Before automating, understand who and what the system affects — prior impact analysis
Decisions must reflect diverse stakeholder input and avoid concentration of automated authority

Avoid deliberate misuse of data and enable timely correction of erroneous data
Data processing must not reproduce algorithmic bias or opacity in deep-learning models
Protect personal data throughout the AI lifecycle
AI systems must be safe and not generate risks to social structures and fundamental rights
Technical neutrality does not exist — systems must be governed against algorithmic bias and opacity
Right to information must not be compromised by automation mechanisms

Tripartite risk classification of AI systems: prohibited (uso indebido), high-risk (riesgo alto), acceptable (riesgo aceptable)
Prohibited: deceptive or manipulative influence on human decision-making using subliminal techniques or exploitation of vulnerabilities
Prohibited: lethal autonomous systems that make decisions without human supervision causing physical harm
Prohibited: inference of sensitive attributes (race, politics, religion, sexual orientation) from biometric data
Prohibited: crime prediction based on personality profiling or trait assessment
High-risk classification: critical infrastructure, education, employment, social programs, credit, healthcare, emotion inference

Mitigation measures when risks detected: model adjustments, data-quality improvement, human-oversight mechanisms
Documentation retention for private impact assessments: minimum 3 years, available to judicial/administrative authorities
Prior, clear, and simple notification to users of AI system purpose, functionality, and decision types

Explanation of automated decisions affecting human rights, in accessible language with key criteria and factors
Privacy by design: data minimization, anonymization techniques integrated into AI system development (Art. 29.b)
Bias identification and minimization using best practices, national/international standards
Risk management: encryption, anomaly detection, model robustness measures for AI systems
Security audits: mandatory before deployment and periodic during operation, identifying vulnerabilities and biases

Maintain human oversight and named accountability for AI systems in regulated sectors
Develop adaptive policy frameworks and standards on Data & AI including ethical use of AI

Safety — conduct impact/risk assessments and ensure known risks are identified and mitigated (process checks)

Government shall plan risk response measures (Art. 19 ¶1)
Government shall establish usage guidelines or internal control management mechanisms (Art. 19 ¶2)
Ensure internal governance responsibilities and external social responsibilities are assumed (Art. 4(7))
Prohibition on AI manipulation to incite self-harm
Prohibition on AI manipulation to harm others
Prohibition on AI manipulation to encourage criminal activity
Prohibition on government social scoring systems
Prohibition on government biometric data capture without consent
Prohibition on AI designed to infringe constitutional rights
Prohibition on AI designed for unlawful discrimination
Prohibition on AI for child pornography production/distribution
Prohibition on AI simulating child sexual conduct
Government agency AI disclosure to consumers before/at interaction
Disclosure must be clear, conspicuous, plain language, no dark patterns
Healthcare AI disclosure timing — by date service first provided (emergency: ASAP)
Rebuttable presumption of reasonable care for NIST AI RMF compliance
AI systems should function in a robust, secure and safe way throughout the AI life cycle
Risks should be continually identified, assessed and managed
AI systems should be technically secure and reliably function as intended
System developers should embed resilience to security threats at each life-cycle stage
Regulators may require regular testing / due diligence on functioning, resilience and security
Kill-switch / halt capability for unsafe AI behaviour
Communicate appropriate information about an AI system (when, how, for which purposes it is used) to relevant people
Decision-making processes should be accessible, interpretable and understandable (explainability)
Transparency/explainability should be proportionate to the risk presented by the AI system
AI systems should not discriminate unfairly against individuals
Governance measures should ensure effective oversight of the supply and use of AI systems
Clear lines of accountability established across the AI life cycle
AI life cycle actors should adhere to the principles and implement measures at all stages
Clear expectations for regulatory compliance placed on appropriate actors in the supply chain
Assurance techniques (e.g. impact assessments) should identify risks early in the life cycle
Organisations ensure proper functioning of AI systems they develop, deploy, or operate
Users, impacted third parties and life-cycle actors should be able to contest an AI decision or outcome that is harmful
Appropriate transparency and explainability support contestability implementation
Cross-sectoral risk assessment — identify and prioritise new and emerging AI risks
Respect, protect, and promote human rights, fundamental freedoms, and human dignity throughout the AI life cycle (para 13–16)
Recognise, protect, and promote environment and ecosystem flourishing; reduce carbon footprint and environmental impact of AI systems (para 17–18)
Proportionality and do no harm — AI use must not exceed what is necessary for a legitimate aim; risk assessment procedures must be implemented (para 25–26)
Safety and security — unwanted harms and vulnerabilities to attack must be avoided, prevented, and eliminated throughout the AI life cycle (para 27)
Fairness and non-discrimination — minimise and avoid reinforcing biased applications and outcomes; effective remedy against discrimination must be available (para 28–30)
Sustainability — continuous assessment of human, social, cultural, economic, and environmental impact of AI technologies (para 31)
Right to privacy and data protection — privacy must be respected throughout the AI life cycle; adequate data protection frameworks and privacy impact assessments (para 32–34)
Human oversight and determination — ethical and legal responsibility must always be attributable to physical persons or legal entities; life-and-death decisions must not be ceded to AI (para 35–36)
Transparency and explainability — people must be informed when decisions are AI-informed; individuals should access reasons for decisions and request review (para 37–41)
Responsibility and accountability — ethical and legal responsibility must always be attributable to AI actors; oversight, audit, and due-diligence mechanisms must be developed (para 42–43)
Introduce frameworks for ethical impact assessments to identify benefits, concerns, risks, and mitigation measures for AI systems (para 50)
Develop due diligence and oversight mechanisms to identify, prevent, mitigate, and account for AI system impacts on human rights and rule of law (para 51)
Monitor all phases of the AI life cycle, including algorithm functioning, data, and AI actors involved (para 52)
Investigate and redress harms caused through AI systems; promote auditability and traceability (para 55)
Develop accountability and liability frameworks ensuring ultimate responsibility lies with natural or legal persons; AI systems must not be given legal personality (para 68)
Develop data governance strategies ensuring continual evaluation of training data quality, data security, and protection measures (para 71)
Put in place safeguards to protect the right to privacy; carry out privacy impact assessments and apply privacy by design (para 72)
Assess direct and indirect environmental impact throughout the AI life cycle including carbon footprint, energy consumption, and raw material extraction (para 84)
Favour data-, energy-, and resource-efficient AI methods; apply precautionary principle where disproportionate negative impacts exist (para 86)
Ensure AI systems in health care are safe, effective, scientifically proven, and that final diagnosis and treatment decisions are taken by humans (para 121–123)
Ensure users can easily identify whether they are interacting with a living being or an AI system and can request human intervention (para 127)
Regulate human-robot interactions; ensure AI-powered neurotechnologies and brain-computer interfaces preserve human dignity and autonomy (para 125–126)
Credibly and transparently monitor and evaluate policies, programmes, and mechanisms related to ethics of AI using quantitative and qualitative approaches (para 131)
Develop tools and indicators for assessing effectiveness and efficiency against agreed standards; monitoring should be continuous and proportionate to risk (para 133)
Disclosure upon request — clearly and conspicuously disclose AI interaction when asked in consumer-protection-regulated activities
Regulated occupation disclosure — prominently disclose when interacting with generative AI in regulated services
Verbal disclosure timing — at start of oral exchange for regulated occupations
Electronic disclosure timing — before written exchange for regulated occupations
Prevent harm from the use of augmented and other AI in health care and human services
Right to mental and neural data privacy
Right to change an individual's decision regarding neurotechnology and to determine by what means to change that decision
Right to protection from neurotechnological interventions of the mind and from unauthorized access to or manipulation of an individual's brain activity
Right to protection from unauthorized neurotechnological alterations in mental functions critical to personality
Right to confirm whether controller is processing personal data and to access such data
Right to opt out of processing for targeted advertising, sale of personal data, or profiling with legal/significant effects
Data minimization — limit collection to what is adequate, relevant, and reasonably necessary
Purpose limitation — no processing incompatible with disclosed purposes without consent
Establish and maintain reasonable administrative, technical, and physical data security practices
Secure, authenticated means for consumers to submit rights requests; no new account required
Obtain consumer consent before processing sensitive data (race, religion, health, sexual orientation, genetic/biometric, child data, precise geolocation)
No targeted advertising, sale, or profiling of children's data without parental consent per COPPA (§ 59.1-578.F)
No sale or offer for sale of precise geolocation data (2026 Chapter 820 amendment, § 59.1-578.A.6)
Data protection assessment for profiling with foreseeable risk of unfair/deceptive treatment, disparate impact, injury, or intrusion (§ 59.1-580.A.3)
Data protection assessment for sensitive data processing and heightened-risk processing activities (§ 59.1-580.A.4–5)
Data protection assessment for online services directed to children; weigh benefits vs risks, factor de-identified data and consumer expectations (§ 59.1-580.B–C)
Processor adheres to controller instructions and assists with consumer rights requests
Processor provides information for DPAs and allows controller or independent assessor assessments
